questions.question: Prove that Information Gain is always non-negative, i.e., IG(S, A) ≥ 0 for any attribute A.
keyPoints: Start with definition: IG(S,A) = H(S) - Σ(|Sᵥ|/|S|)H(Sᵥ)
Apply convexity property: For 0 ≤ pᵢ ≤ 1, ΣpᵢH(Sᵥ) ≤ H(S)
This inequality holds because entropy function is concave
Since H(S) ≥ weighted sum of child entropies, IG ≥ 0
Equality holds only when H(Sᵥ) = H(S) for all v (no impurity reduction)

questions.question: Explain the bias-variance tradeoff in the context of Decision Tree splitting criteria (Entropy/Gini).
keyPoints: Gini tends to isolate the most frequent class in its own branch (more aggressive splitting)
Entropy tends to produce more balanced trees (more uniform probability distributions)
Gini has lower computational cost (no logarithms)
Entropy provides better probabilistic interpretation
Choice affects tree depth and generalization: Gini may overfit, Entropy may underfit

questions.question: Derive the formula for Variance Reduction in regression trees and explain its role in splitting.
keyPoints: Total Variance = Σ(yᵢ - ȳ)² where ȳ is mean of y values
Variance Reduction = Total Variance - Σ(wᵢ × Variance(Sᵢ)) where wᵢ = |Sᵢ|/|S|
The split that maximizes variance reduction is selected
Child nodes have lower variance than parent, indicating more homogeneous regions
This is analogous to Information Gain but for continuous target variables

questions.question: Compare and contrast Pre-pruning and Post-pruning strategies for decision trees with appropriate examples.
keyPoints: Pre-pruning: Stop tree growth early using stopping criteria (max depth, min samples, gain threshold)
Post-pruning: Build full tree, then remove/substitute subtrees
Example pre-pruning: Stop when node has <10 samples
Example post-pruning: Replace subtree with leaf if validation error doesn't increase
Tradeoffs: Pre-pruning faster but may miss complex patterns; post-pruning more accurate but slower

questions.question: Explain why Gain Ratio is preferred over Information Gain in C4.5 algorithm with a numerical example.
keyPoints: Information Gain has bias toward attributes with many values
Example: Attribute with unique ID for each sample has maximum IG but is useless
Gain Ratio = IG / SplitInfo normalizes by attribute's intrinsic information
SplitInfo measures entropy of split distribution (higher for many values)
This penalizes attributes with many nearly-equal sized splits

questions.question:
For the dataset below, calculate which attribute (A or B) would be selected for the first split using Information Gain:
- Class distribution: 6 positive, 6 negative
- Attribute A splits into: A1 (4 pos, 1 neg), A2 (2 pos, 5 neg)
- Attribute B splits into: B1 (3 pos, 3 neg), B2 (3 pos, 3 neg)
keyPoints: Calculate parent entropy: H(S) = -[0.5×log₂(0.5) + 0.5×log₂(0.5)] = 1.0
For A: Weighted avg = (5/12)×H(A1) + (7/12)×H(A2)
For B: H(B1)=H(B2)=1.0, weighted avg=1.0, IG(B)=0
Attribute A selected (higher IG)

questions.question: Provide detailed pseudocode for the ID3 algorithm and analyze its time and space complexity. Also explain how C4.5 extends ID3 to handle continuous attributes and missing values.
keyPoints: ID3 pseudocode: Recursively select attribute with highest IG, create node, branch on values, stop when all samples same class or no attributes left
Time complexity: O(n×d×log n) where n=samples, d=features for sorting + O(n×d) for IG calculation per node
Space complexity: O(n×d) for storing data + O(depth) recursion stack
C4.5 continuous: Sort values, try midpoints as thresholds, evaluate IG for each
C4.5 missing: Assign weight to samples (fractional counts), distribute proportionally to known distributions

questions.question: Derive and explain the mathematical relationship between Entropy, Gini Impurity, and Classification Error. Show that they are all measures of impurity that satisfy the same basic properties.
keyPoints: Entropy: H(S) = -Σpᵢlog₂(pᵢ), range [0, log₂(c)]
Gini: G(S) = 1 - Σpᵢ², range [0, 1-1/c]
Classification Error: E(S) = 1 - max(pᵢ), range [0, 1-1/c]
All three: 0 when pᵢ=1 for some i (pure), maximum at uniform distribution
They differ in sensitivity: Error is least sensitive, Entropy most sensitive to probability changes
All are concave functions satisfying: f(0)=f(1)=0, f is maximum at 1/c, symmetric

questions.question: Explain the CART algorithm in detail including: (a) binary splitting strategy, (b) Gini index as splitting criterion for classification, (c) variance reduction for regression, (d) cost-complexity pruning.
keyPoints: (a) CART always creates binary splits, even for categorical: one branch satisfies condition, other doesn't
(b) Gini(S) = 1 - Σpᵢ², select attribute that maximizes Gini reduction: ΔG = G(parent) - Σ(wᵢ×G(childᵢ))
(c) Regression: minimize weighted variance, MSE reduction = σ²(parent) - Σ(wᵢ×σ²(childᵢ))
(d) Cost-complexity: Minimize Rα(T) = R(T) + α|T| where R(T)=total misclassification rate, |T|=leaves, α=complexity parameter
Find optimal α via cross-validation, prune iteratively from leaves
